title: Kayeu Chan Timro Lagi Pani
subtitle: There are more hearts that quietly hold you than you'll ever know.
caption: A gentle reminder that even when love feels lost, you are still cherished in countless unseen ways.
fullPoem:
Kayeu chan timro lagi pani.

Ghumayeko prem,
Payera pani harayeka yaad haru,
Farkauna khojne timi.

Bigat thiyo ra ta timi chau,
Ani timi chau ra ta yi yaad haru chan.
Yei yaad ma bacheka hau,
Tinlai birsina chai nakhojnu hai timi.

U farki aaune aasha ta didina ma,
Tara ekai juni na ho,
Bheti po halchau ki kunai din.

U nafarke pani,
Farkine aasha chai namarnu hai timi.

Biteka din nai ta hun,
Samjhi musukka muskurai dinu.
Samjhaunu aafno man lai,
Ani samjhinu,
Aafu pani ek palta timi.

Kayeu chan timro lagi pani.
meaning: This poem is about hope after heartbreak. It reminds us that although some people leave and some memories remain unfinished, our lives are still filled with people who quietly care for us. Even when love seems absent, kindness, gratitude, and affection continue to exist in places we may never notice.
authorNotes: I wrote this for everyone who has ever believed they were unloved. Sometimes we become unforgettable to people without ever realizing it.
inspiration: The realization that we are remembered not only by those we loved, but also by strangers, friends, teachers, and people whose lives we touched for only a moment.
seoTitle: Kayeu Chan Timro Lagi Pani | A Poem About Hope and Quiet Love
seoDescription: A heartfelt Nepali spoken-word poem reminding us that we are loved, remembered, and cherished in more ways than we often realize.
altText: A warm golden sunset with a solitary figure smiling gently, symbolizing hope after heartbreak.

title: Bilauney Chan Yi Yaad Haru
subtitle: Parkhai ko pachhiltira, sayau sapana pachhadai...
caption: A farewell to thirteen years of memories, friendships, classrooms, and the place that quietly became home.
fullPoem:
Parkhai ko pachhiltira,
Sayau sapana pachhadai,
Parkhaune ra parkhirahaneharu ko lagi...

Yaad rakhnu,
Bilauney chan yi yaad haru.

Aajhai pani samjhinchu ma tyo Saraswati Pooja ko din,
Aankha bhari aashu,
Mero haat samatera ka, kha, ga lekhna sikaaunu bhayeko
Mero aadarniya guru.

Yi harsha ra sangharsha le bhariyeka 13 barsaka yaad,
Aba sayad samjhanama matrai sajiyera basne chan.

Feri eutai kakshyama basera,
Eutailai jiskaudai hasne din kaha farkela ra?

Samaya farke na farke,
Aba antyama sabai sadhai sangai hune bacha haru pani chutine chan.

Adhura maya ka katha,
Mitratako bichhod,
Yi sabai le kehi amulya paath sikayeko cha.

Suru bhayeko kura ta ekdin antya hune nai rahecha.
Aba antyama,
Chhodi janu nai parne rahecha.

Chhodi jana sakula ra kasari?
Rakhana na malai yetai katai.

Basaune bas nai rahena bhane pani,
Samjhana mai samhalnu hai malai.
meaning: This poem is a farewell to school, childhood, teachers, friendships, and the memories that quietly shape us. It reflects on how ordinary moments become priceless only after they have passed, reminding us that every ending leaves behind something worth carrying forever.
authorNotes: This was one of the first poems I ever shared publicly. It was inspired by leaving school and realizing that the place I once wanted to leave had become the place I missed the most.
inspiration: School farewell, Bhanu Smarika writings, teachers, friendships, and the bittersweet feeling of growing up.
seoTitle: Bilauney Chan Yi Yaad Haru | A Nepali Farewell Poem
seoDescription: A heartfelt Nepali poem about school memories, friendship, growing up, and the people we never truly leave behind.
altText: A nostalgic school corridor at sunset, symbolizing memories and farewell.

title: Haraeka Aasha Ani Aastha
subtitle: Faith should never become a weapon against humanity.
caption: A spoken-word poem questioning the exploitation of faith and the suffering justified in the name of God.
quote: Aakhir antyama ti phool chadhauneharu pani ta metidai jane rahechhan.
fullPoem:
Haraeka aasha ani aastha.

Bhanchhan Ishwar parkhaune ani timi parkhane. Tara parkhinda parkhinda pani timi parkhana chai nachhodnu hai. Thalima phool sajaai rakhnu timi. Bholi mrityupachhi pani arko juni jiune prayatna prapta garnu chha re.

Devata mandina ta bhandina ma, tara Saraswati puji dhanko aasha rakhchhau timi sayad. Bhagwanko aastha nai nabhaidiyeko bhaye ek pujariko lagi aafno dhan khera faldainthyau hola timile. Sharir usko ghisriyer kharani hudai chha ra ajhai pani kayaule aafno dhoopko dhuwaamai dhalma lyaudai Ishwarko jawafko lagi parkhirahichhan. Aafno swarthale Bhagwanko murtibhitra bere bechne uni.

Bhakal garna lanchhu bhandai mandirkai agadi kayau nari sopidieka chhan tinile. Aaja ek barsha bhaisakyo. Ahilesamma pani Sarita didiko kapadabat dhusi lageko ragat ganaaunchha. Dharmako naamma ti adharmile unko aasha namaridiyeko bhaye sayad, sayad aaja Sarita didi jiundai po hunthin ki?

Aakhir antyama ti phool chadhauneharu pani ta metidai jane rahechhan.
meaning: This poem is not an attack on faith or religion. Instead, it questions how belief can be manipulated by people for power, profit, and control. It asks whether humanity is sometimes forgotten when rituals become more important than compassion, and reminds us that no act committed in the name of God can justify injustice.
authorNotes: I wrote this to separate faith from exploitation. The poem challenges the misuse of religion while respecting the personal beliefs of those who practice it with kindness and humanity.
inspiration: Stories of people who suffered because trust, devotion, and religious authority were abused, and the realization that compassion should always stand above blind obedience.
seoTitle: Haraeka Aasha Ani Aastha | A Nepali Spoken Word on Faith and Humanity
seoDescription: A thought-provoking Nepali spoken-word poem examining the misuse of faith, religious exploitation, and the importance of compassion over blind belief.
altText: A dimly lit temple with fading flowers and incense smoke, symbolizing faith, loss, and reflection.

title: Samjhanako Sajh
subtitle: Every sunset carries a dream that chose to become a memory.
caption: A poem about accepting unfinished dreams and finding peace in the paths destiny quietly rewrites.
quote: Gham pani ta astaunu parchha... hami kina nasakne?
fullPoem:
Samjhanako sajh...

Kahile kahi,
Jindagile socheko bato roj्दaina.
Hami sapana buniranchhau,
Tara bhagyele afnai kahani lekhiraheko hunchha.

Kati sapana adhurai rahanchan,
Kati ichchha samayale sangai lagidinchha.
Tara adhurai bhayeka sapanaharu pani,
Hamilai banauchan.

Astaudai gareko ghamlai hera,
Usle pani afno ujyalo chhadera janu parchha.
Tara bholi feri udaya hunchha.

Tyasaile,
Adhurapan sanga darau nakhos.
Kehi sapana pura hunchan,
Kehi samjhana banchan.

Ra kahile kahi,
Samjhanako sajh nai,
Jeevanako sabai bhanda sundar rang hunchha.
meaning: This poem reflects on the dreams we couldn't fulfill and the reality that destiny sometimes leads us somewhere unexpected. Rather than seeing unfinished dreams as failures, it encourages us to embrace them as beautiful parts of our journey. Just as every sunset marks an ending before another sunrise, every ending carries the possibility of a new beginning.
authorNotes: I wrote this after realizing that not every dream is meant to come true. Some dreams exist only to shape who we become, and there is beauty in accepting that.
inspiration: The setting sun, unfinished dreams, and the quiet realization that destiny often writes a different story than the one we imagined.
seoTitle: Samjhanako Sajh | A Nepali Poem About Unfinished Dreams
seoDescription: A heartfelt Nepali spoken-word poem about accepting destiny, unfinished dreams, and discovering beauty in every ending.
altText: A golden sunset over distant hills, symbolizing hope, acceptance, and the beauty of unfinished dreams.

title: Tuteka Tara
subtitle: Tuteka tara pani ta antim ma bilaune rahechan.
caption: A poem about the quiet terror of being forgotten after the last person who remembers you is gone.
quote: Ma sarir matra pani ta thiena... tara aaja tyo sarir bina, ma ko nai po hu ra?
fullPoem:
Tuteka tara pani ta antim ma bilaune rahechan.

Ma aafno parchhai ma aaja harayeko chu.
Aarighaatka aagoma udera gaeko dhuwajastai,
ma pani kahilekai bilayera jaanchhu.

Ma mareko din ma,
malai samjhine timi pani ek din bilauchau.
Ani tespachi,
ma ko smritima baachne?

Biteka rituharu aaja bihani parkadai chan.
Harek raat ma eklai hune tara haru,
aaja afai eklai chan.

Ma banchhu,
bujhchu,
ani bistarai ma afai bilaunchhu.

Ma sarir matra pani ta thiena.
Tara aaja,
tyo sarir bina,
ma...
ko nai po hu ra?
meaning: This poem confronts the inevitability of being forgotten. It reflects on how every relationship, every memory, and even the people who remember us will eventually disappear. When the final witness to our existence is gone, the poem asks whether any part of us truly remains. It is less about death itself and more about the fragile nature of identity, memory, and human existence.
authorNotes: Written while reflecting on the unsettling realization that one day, even the people who remember us will no longer be here. The poem became an exploration of what remains when memory itself dies.
inspiration: The thought that everyone eventually experiences a 'second death'—the moment the last person who remembers them is gone.
seoTitle: Tuteka Tara | A Poem on Memory, Death & Identity
seoDescription: A Nepali spoken-word poem exploring memory, mortality, identity, and the quiet fear of being forgotten.
altText: A solitary night sky with a fading broken star symbolizing memory and impermanence.

title: Smritiko Jhyalale Sanchera Rakheko Hawa
subtitle: Some souls leave our lives, but never our hearts.
caption: A poem about the kind of love that never truly disappears, even when the person does.
quote: Sakne bhaye sakne bhanda pani badhi... ma sadaiv timrai chhayale rangaauna khojnechhu.
fullPoem:
Smritiko jhyalale sanchera rakheko hawa,
Aaja arkai batassanga magn chha.

Dulechhu feri.
Tyahi aakashkai aankhabhitra haraeko aasu,
Sayad hawakai batasanga bilaudai chhan.

Aasu dhune kramma,
Aasu birseka ti mayaka malami,
Timi timibhitra nai haraeko chhau aaja.

Jaleko pida,
Ragatle bhije pani,
Rang ta ajhai jiwit nai ta chha.

Sakne bhaye,
Sakne bhanda pani badhi,
Tyahi rittiyeko samarpanma bhijeko ragatle bhaye pani,
Ma sadaiv timrai chhayale rangaauna khojnechhu.

Biralo baat.
meaning: This poem explores the strange permanence of love. Even after people leave, the emotions they awakened continue to live within us. It reflects on devotion that survives distance, loss, and time—not because we refuse to let go, but because some connections quietly become part of who we are.
authorNotes: I wrote this while reflecting on how memories outlive relationships. Sometimes love doesn't ask to be remembered—it simply remains.
inspiration: The quiet realization that some people become part of our inner world forever, even after they are gone from our lives.
seoTitle: Smritiko Jhyalale Sanchera Rakheko Hawa | A Nepali Poem on Memory and Devotion
seoDescription: A heartfelt Nepali spoken-word poem about memories, quiet devotion, longing, and the kind of love that never truly leaves.
altText: An open window with soft evening light and wind flowing through sheer curtains, symbolizing lingering memories and enduring love.

title: Swarthi Sarkar
subtitle: When power forgets humanity, history remembers.
caption: A spoken-word poem condemning political indifference, corruption, and the cost ordinary people pay for power.
fullPoem:
Kati jhuto bolchhau hai swarthi sarkar!

Sapanako jhyal pachhadi jhundiyeko parchhai aaja banda bakasma bandhiyeka chhan.
Gharma chhoro katikher aula bhani kurera baseki aamako santan tyahi Aareghatko alankarma bhasiyeka chhan aaja.
Pasina bechera paisa kamaeka bubaka bachchaka ragat ra laashma teker rajneeti garchhau.

Taipani chaldai chha tyahi sarkar.
Tara samjhi rakhnu,
Ragatle lekheka kathaharu birsiyeka hunechhainan.
Janatako harek aasu ani aakroshle timro mahal dhwasta parnechhan.
Timiharuko swarthako bishalu bhid kebal antyama ek purano katha matra bannechha.
meaning: This poem is a protest against political selfishness and the human cost of irresponsible leadership. It gives voice to families who bear the consequences of decisions made by those in power and reminds us that suffering and sacrifice are never erased from collective memory. It is ultimately a call for accountability, justice, and compassion.
authorNotes: I wrote this in response to the grief, anger, and helplessness many people feel when ordinary lives are lost while those in power remain distant from the consequences. The poem speaks for the emotions of those left waiting, mourning, and demanding accountability.
inspiration: The pain of grieving families, public frustration after tragedy, and the belief that people should never be forgotten in the pursuit of power.
seoTitle: Swarthi Sarkar | A Nepali Spoken-Word Poem on Justice and Accountability
seoDescription: A powerful Nepali spoken-word poem reflecting on political responsibility, public grief, and the enduring memory of sacrifice.
altText: A dark silhouette of a government building beneath a stormy sky, symbolizing public anger and the demand for accountability.

title: Chad Ki Chakku?
subtitle: When celebration forgets compassion.
caption: A spoken-word poem reflecting on compassion, loss, and the questions we should ask ourselves during our greatest festivals.
quote: Praniko chhati chirne chakkuko dharlai dharma manchhau.
fullPoem:
Dasain aayo are.
Pardeshiko phika man,
Nani aasu jharna nasakeka aankhaka pida kasle bujhidiyun?
Timi, ma, hami ani hamro,
Sayad yahi nai ta jivan hola.

Praniko chhati chirne chakkuko dharlai dharma manchhau.
Ti hajarau prani katida,
Aaja hami afai chharaliyeka chhau.

Aafnai petko bhokle,
Sayad ragatko dharma bachaune tyahi sanskritiko shokle.

Tiharko diyole sajiyeko sadak,
Batoma hidne balika farkana daraune darlagdo andhyaro galli nabanos.

Dasainko bihani,
Kothabhitra thuneki kishori,
Arko barshadekhi bhittepatro heri Dasain aaune din gandai basna naparos.
meaning: This poem reflects on the contrast between the joy of festivals and the suffering that can exist alongside them. It raises questions about compassion, violence, separation, and the responsibilities we carry toward one another. Rather than rejecting tradition, it encourages reflection on how festivals can better embody humanity, safety, and kindness.
authorNotes: I wrote this during Dashain while thinking about the many different realities people experience during the same celebration. For some it is reunion and happiness, while for others it is loneliness, fear, or grief. The poem invites reflection on those unseen experiences.
inspiration: Dashain, migrant families separated by work, compassion for living beings, and the hope that every festival becomes a time of safety and humanity for everyone.
seoTitle: Chad Ki Chakku? | A Nepali Spoken-Word Poem on Festivals and Compassion
seoDescription: A reflective Nepali spoken-word poem exploring compassion, humanity, and the many realities hidden behind our biggest festivals.
altText: A festive street lit with warm lights, contrasted by a quiet, shadowed alley, symbolizing celebration alongside reflection.

title: I Don't Pray to God Anymore
subtitle: Not for miracles, but for the strength to endure.
caption: A spoken-word poem about faith, doubt, resilience, and the courage to keep beginning again.
quote: I won't pray to win, but to lose one more time so that I could succeed again.
fullPoem:
I don't pray to God anymore.

What if He listens?
What if He listened on the days I don't feel like myself,
when I'm a monster for me?

So I won't pray to God,
not for kindness,
not for light,
and not even for goodness.

I won't pray for all that is good,
but for all the bad that's necessary.

If I had to pray for something,
it surely wouldn't be You,
but the strength to fight for You.

Regardless of who I am,
and who I'm praying for,
just or unjust,
if there is a God,
You will surely find me on my knees,
praying.

Praying not for forgiveness,
but for the sanity to start a new journey again.

I won't pray to win,
but to lose one more time,
so that I could succeed again.

Even if I'm on my last days,
I wouldn't pray to leave,
but to pray just a bit longer.
meaning: This poem explores the tension between faith and human struggle. Rather than asking for miracles or an easier life, it reflects on the desire for resilience—the strength to endure hardship, begin again after failure, and continue searching for meaning even in moments of doubt. It is not a rejection of faith but a meditation on perseverance.
authorNotes: This piece was inspired by a philosophical thought rather than a personal declaration. It explores the idea that sometimes the greatest prayer is not for life to become easier, but for the courage to keep facing it.
inspiration: Questions about faith, resilience, human vulnerability, and the quiet determination to continue despite uncertainty.
seoTitle: I Don't Pray to God Anymore | A Spoken-Word Poem on Faith and Resilience
seoDescription: A reflective spoken-word poem exploring faith, doubt, perseverance, and the courage to begin again.
altText: A solitary figure kneeling beneath a vast night sky, symbolizing reflection, resilience, and hope.

title: The Way I Loved
subtitle: Some loves don't stay, but they forever change the heart that held them.
caption: A spoken-word poem about loving without reservation, carrying memories that never quite leave, and discovering that vulnerability is one of the most human things we can do.
quote: Maybe there is nothing more human than this—to be destroyed completely and still call it love.
fullPoem:
I always thought love had an address,
somewhere between two hearts,
two hands trembling,
facing towards each other.

The kind of love that makes your pulse trip over itself
at the sound of someone's name.

And if that's what love ever is all about,
I will forever be choosing the stranger
who handed me his umbrella,
even though the rain wasn't kind to him either.

Because love, I have learned,
and maybe that's the cruel poetry of it all,
it teaches us how to hold the sun
just to remind us
that our hands were never fireproof.

I still catch myself
looking for you
in the small voices of people
that sound almost like yours.

And maybe no one
will ever love me
the way I loved,
like a candle burning
just to prove the darkness wrong.

But if that's all I ever had,
let me make this world know,
I loved until it hurt to breathe.

And maybe there is nothing more human than this—
to be destroyed completely
and still call it love.
meaning: This poem reflects on the beauty and cost of loving deeply. It suggests that love is measured not only by whether it lasts, but by the kindness, vulnerability, and transformation it leaves behind. Even when love ends in heartbreak, the willingness to love wholeheartedly remains one of the most profoundly human experiences.
authorNotes: I wanted to write about love beyond romance—the quiet acts of kindness, the memories that linger, and the courage it takes to keep an open heart even after loss.
inspiration: Love that changes us, fleeting human connections, quiet acts of compassion, and the realization that vulnerability often becomes our greatest strength.
seoTitle: The Way I Loved | A Spoken-Word Poem About Love and Vulnerability
seoDescription: A heartfelt spoken-word poem exploring love, heartbreak, kindness, and the courage to love deeply despite the possibility of loss.
altText: A candle glowing softly in the rain, symbolizing enduring love, hope, and vulnerability.

title: Too Busy To Glow
subtitle: The world still shines. Perhaps we have simply forgotten how to sit beside its light.
caption: A spoken-word poem reflecting on Tihar, the changing pace of life, and how we often lose the warmth that once made ordinary moments feel extraordinary.
quote: Too busy to shine, we almost forgot what it means to glow.
fullPoem:
The earth feels older than it looks today.

The world glows with light,
but the light inside it flickers.

But still,
the earth hasn't changed.
The flowers still bloom.
The sky still listens.

The lamp still burns faithfully,
but it is we
who have become smoke,
drifting,
fading,
and forgetting the way back home.

The marigolds still shine
like little suns,
but they don't smell the same anymore.
Maybe it's not them.
Maybe it's us,
running too fast
to stand in their softness.

There are more lights than ever,
but less glow
in the hearts that light them.

The world still glows,
maybe just not the way it used to.
But it isn't the earth
that grew colder.

It's us,
who have stopped sitting near the fire.
Too busy to shine,
we almost forgot
what it means to glow.
meaning: This poem reflects on how modern life often pulls us away from the quiet beauty of ordinary moments. While nature, festivals, and traditions continue to offer warmth and light, we sometimes become too hurried to notice them. It invites us to slow down, reconnect with home, and rediscover the inner glow that comes from presence rather than constant motion.
authorNotes: I wrote this during Tihar while noticing how brightly homes were decorated, yet how easily we rush through moments that once brought us together. The poem became a reminder to pause and truly be present.
inspiration: Tihar lamps, marigold flowers, evenings at home, and the feeling that although the world still offers beauty, we often move too quickly to receive it.
seoTitle: Too Busy To Glow | A Spoken-Word Poem on Tihar, Home, and Presence
seoDescription: A reflective spoken-word poem inspired by Tihar, exploring how modern life can distance us from warmth, tradition, and the quiet beauty of simply being present.
altText: Rows of glowing oil lamps and marigold flowers at dusk, symbolizing warmth, home, and reflection.

title: Ko Hau Timi?
subtitle: If a rose is remembered for its fragrance, what is it that I have?
caption: A spoken-word poem about identity, purpose, and the quiet search for one's place in an ever-changing world.
quote: Gulabko jasto subas ma ma kaha chha ra?
fullPoem:
Ko hau timi?
Ko hu ma?
Ke chha ra ma?
Ma... ra kaska lagi lekhdaichhu yo sab?

Nadi,
Nala,
Nahar,
Khola,
Ra sagar...
Ta bas eutaa bahana hun.

Ghamko taapbat tataeka pani,
Ra tataeka panibat baneka ti badal.

Asar mase jharima barsine barshako pani hu ma.

Ghamko taapma murjhaera baseko phool,
Ra kehi kshanko andhakarlai raat manera basne bhul hu ma.

Bhanna ta,
Nana shahar ghumdai hidne sapana chhan mera,
Tara gharbat bahira duldai,
Galliharuma dekhine chhaina.

Rukh,
Pahad,
Himal,
Khola,
Ra tyo bagaichama phuliyeka sundar gulabko phool...

Bhanne sapana thie,
Dekhe thien,
Tara...

Gulabko jasto subas,
Ma ma kaha chha ra?
meaning: This poem is a meditation on identity and belonging. Through images of rivers, rain, mountains, and flowers, it reflects on how life is constantly changing while we continue searching for our own place within it. The poem questions whether we possess anything truly memorable or meaningful, leaving the reader with the quiet realization that the search for purpose is itself part of being human.
authorNotes: I wrote this while questioning who I was beyond the words I wrote. Rather than trying to answer those questions, I wanted the poem to sit with them, allowing uncertainty itself to become part of the story.
inspiration: Nature, the water cycle, fleeting dreams, ordinary life, and the feeling of searching for one's own identity in a world that is always moving.
seoTitle: Ko Hau Timi? | A Nepali Spoken-Word Poem on Identity and Purpose
seoDescription: A reflective Nepali spoken-word poem exploring identity, dreams, purpose, and the search for meaning through nature and self-questioning.
altText: A solitary figure standing beside a river beneath a cloudy sky, symbolizing introspection, identity, and the search for purpose.

title: Birsine Bhayen
subtitle: Some people leave, but their memories learn how to stay.
caption: A spoken-word poem about unfinished love, memory, and the quiet promise that some people can never truly be forgotten.
quote: Bhuliye pani birsina nasakine mero adhuro yaad hau timi.
fullPoem:
Sangaalera rakheki chhu,
Saanchoko saanch maya.

Yaad ra astitwako bato nai farak bhae pani,
Nabirsidinu hai.

Bujhera birsane juni na ho.

Birsane bela,
Bhulieka dinharu,
Ani,
Samjhanako baaki shabdaharu
Samjhidinu.

Bhuliye pani,
Birsina nasakine,
Mero,
Adhuro yaad hau timi.
meaning: This poem reflects on the kind of love that remains alive through memory even after people part ways. It accepts that distance, time, and changing lives may separate two hearts, yet some connections become inseparable from who we are. Rather than asking to be remembered forever, it quietly hopes that what was once genuine will never be completely erased.
authorNotes: I wanted this poem to feel like a quiet goodbye rather than a farewell filled with regret. Sometimes love doesn't disappear—it simply changes into memory, and memory becomes another way of loving.
inspiration: Incomplete love, cherished memories, the passage of time, and the realization that some people become permanent chapters in our lives.
seoTitle: Birsine Bhayen | A Nepali Spoken-Word Poem About Love and Memory
seoDescription: A heartfelt Nepali spoken-word poem about unfinished love, memories that never fade, and the quiet beauty of remembering.
altText: A faded handwritten letter resting beside dried flowers, symbolizing memories, unfinished love, and nostalgia.

title: I Love Love
subtitle: Love isn't always romance. Sometimes it's simply being seen.
caption: A spoken-word poem celebrating the quiet, ordinary forms of love that often go unnoticed—found in strangers, friends, family, teachers, and even within ourselves.
quote: I can't stop myself from loving love every day.
fullPoem:
I love love,
but not the theatrical kind
that drowns itself in dramatic confessions
and calls it devotion.

But the kind of love
that notices the almost invisible,
awkward hesitation
before you said you were okay.

The blurry meme
sent to you by your friend at two in the morning,
carrying nothing but the memory
of how you used to laugh in primary school.

The guy
who gifted you a wooden mug
simply because brown
was your favorite color.

The teacher
who pretended not to notice
your trembling hands,
and how quickly you wiped away your tears
before entering the classroom.

The stranger on a bus
who insisted you take their seat,
even though the exhaustion
looked heavier on their shoulders than yours.

The kind of love
that made me realize
it is better to feel everything
than to learn how to feel nothing.

And even when I know
it isn't always returned the same way,
I can't stop myself
from loving love
 every day.
meaning: This poem challenges the belief that love exists only in romance. It celebrates the quiet acts of care that often pass unnoticed—a thoughtful gesture from a stranger, a teacher's understanding, a friend's late-night message, or the warmth of family. These small moments reveal that love is woven into everyday life, and choosing to notice them is what keeps our hearts open.
authorNotes: I wrote this because I wanted to remind people that love isn't measured only by relationships. Some of the purest forms of love arrive quietly, without expectation, and disappear before we even realize how much they changed us.
inspiration: The unnoticed kindness of strangers, friends who remember small details, supportive teachers, family, and the countless ordinary moments that quietly remind us we are loved.
seoTitle: I Love Love | A Spoken-Word Poem About Everyday Kindness
seoDescription: A heartfelt spoken-word poem exploring love beyond romance, celebrating the quiet acts of kindness that make us feel seen and connected.
altText: A warm evening scene with friends, strangers, and glowing lights, symbolizing the many forms of love found in everyday life.

title: Antim Saajh
subtitle: Even memory forgets. Perhaps that is what evening has always been teaching us.
caption: A spoken-word poem reflecting on impermanence, borrowed homes, fading memories, and the quiet truth that everything eventually returns to where it belongs.
quote: Jaba samjhanale pani mero naam metnechha... ma samjhanechhu.
fullPoem:
Baato chhutedekhi kasto...

Ani...
Antim saajh,
Jaba samjhanale pani mero naam metnechha,
Ma samjhanechhu.

Rukhka kagale udharo diyeko,
Chara farkane nyaano aakashko saapati,
Ma teslai ghar thaanthe.

Tara,
Ritusangai,
Paatharu pani ta,
Aafno basai pherne rahechhan.

Nadile,
Aafno aawaj udharo dii,
Pyaas metna khojthe,
Tara,
Bagdai bagdai,
Mero shabda kinarmai chhodi,
Samudrasanga waacha pura garna hatarine rahechha.

Ma afai,
Udharolai,
Ujyalo jastai,
Ananta basai,
Ani kehi chhed baato dine,
Badalko baslai,
Sayad,
Aafnai ghar thaane rahechhu.

Khula satya bolne ho bhane,
Kati aasu bagne chhan yaha.
meaning: This poem reflects on the impermanence of life and the human tendency to mistake borrowed moments for permanent homes. Through images of migrating birds, changing seasons, flowing rivers, and fading memories, it suggests that everything we hold eventually moves on. Even our names may one day disappear from memory, yet the act of living, remembering, and loving still carries meaning.
authorNotes: I wrote this while thinking about how quietly life changes. We often believe people, places, and moments belong to us forever, only to realize they were simply passing through our lives. This poem became my conversation with that truth.
inspiration: Evening skies, changing seasons, rivers finding the sea, migrating birds, and the realization that nothing in life is truly ours forever.
seoTitle: Antim Saajh | A Nepali Spoken-Word Poem on Memory and Impermanence
seoDescription: A reflective Nepali spoken-word poem exploring memory, borrowed homes, changing seasons, and the quiet impermanence of life.
altText: A lone figure beneath an evening sky beside a flowing river, symbolizing memory, farewell, and the passage of time.

title: An Ordinary Dayy
subtitle: There is no such thing as an ordinary day.
caption: A spoken-word poem about perspective, shared humanity, and the invisible endings and beginnings happening around us every day.
quote: The world ends every day, maybe not for everyone, but for someone, somewhere.
fullPoem:
I heard a quote that says,
"Your worst day is someone's best one."

It made me wonder
how the stars I'm admiring tonight
may already be dead tomorrow,
and someday
the same will be true of us.

While I sit here
thinking about my own life,
billions of other people
are still struggling with equal death,
equal pain,
and equal wonder,
as if the world were made up of countless universes
pretending to share the same sky.

At this very moment,
a dream is coming true
while another is falling apart.
Someone is staring at the same sky,
wishing for the life another is desperate to escape.

The world ends every day,
maybe not for everyone,
but for someone, somewhere,
a friendship dies,
a first love ends,
a chapter closes.

And yet,
the world still continues
to call it
an ordinary day.
meaning: This poem reflects on the hidden significance of everyday life. What appears ordinary from a distance may contain profound joy, grief, loss, or transformation for someone else. It invites us to see the world with greater empathy, recognizing that every day holds countless endings and beginnings happening quietly beyond our awareness.
authorNotes: I wrote this after hearing a simple quote that stayed with me for days. The poem became a reminder that ordinary days are often filled with invisible stories, and that perspective can completely change the meaning of a moment.
inspiration: Night skies, philosophical reflections, the interconnectedness of human lives, and the realization that every day is extraordinary to someone.
seoTitle: An Ordinary Dayy | A Spoken-Word Poem on Perspective and Humanity
seoDescription: A reflective spoken-word poem exploring empathy, perspective, and the extraordinary stories hidden within seemingly ordinary days.
altText: A person sitting beneath a night sky filled with stars, symbolizing reflection, humanity, and the unseen stories of everyday life.

title: Kaha Chha Ra Khusi?
subtitle: Where does happiness survive when life itself is burning?
caption: A spoken-word poem questioning where happiness can truly exist amid loss, poverty, longing, and the fragile nature of human life.
quote: Kaha hola ra khusi? Aaja tyahi jaldai gareko laashma.
fullPoem:
Basantako pahilo hawale nai
Metaidine yo kharanimathi koriyeko kathama.

Chhayamai,
Khusi lukauna khojda,
Sapanaharai lukdathye.

Ma,
Taraaharu panchhaunthe ni,
Ti chamkiraheka saara.

Tara,
Ti chamkiraheka taraaharuko ujyalo nai
Chhindainathe.

Bhidai bhidko bhidbich,
Bhidieka muskaanka makhautaharu.

Shunyatako aakashmuni,
Khojne tyahi khusi,
Kina chahiyo ra aba?

Ghar nahunelai,
Gharko chhanaakai pir.

Nahunelai,
Basaiko jalyai nai rahyo ra.

Maatako malma,
Dhunga ra gitti.

Kaha hola ra khusi?
Aaja,
Tyahi Aareghatko phantma.

Aagoko taapma,
Ani tyahi pulne gareko raagma.

Kaha hola ra khusi?
Aaja,
Tyahi jaldai gareko laashma.

Gahiro...
Gahiro...
Jivan saagar yo...
Tyahi nai rahe.
meaning: This poem reflects on the difficult search for happiness in a world where grief, poverty, and mortality are constant realities. It contrasts dreams with disappointment, crowded smiles with inner emptiness, and finally asks whether happiness can be found even in the presence of death. Rather than offering an answer, it invites the listener to confront the fragility of human existence and question what truly gives life meaning.
authorNotes: I wrote this while thinking about how differently people experience the same world. For some, happiness feels close enough to touch. For others, survival itself becomes the only hope. The poem doesn't try to define happiness—it only asks where we continue searching for it.
inspiration: The contrast between hope and suffering, cremation grounds, crowded cities, poverty, and the quiet realization that life and loss often exist side by side.
seoTitle: Kaha Chha Ra Khusi? | A Nepali Spoken-Word Poem on Happiness and Mortality
seoDescription: A contemplative Nepali spoken-word poem exploring happiness, grief, mortality, and the search for meaning in a fragile world.
altText: A lone figure standing near a quiet cremation ground at dusk beneath a vast sky, symbolizing grief, reflection, and the search for happiness.

title: Mero Desh, Timi Feri Uthnu Chha
subtitle: A nation survives when its people refuse to lose hope.
caption: A spoken-word poem reflecting on sacrifice, inequality, and the enduring hope that a nation can heal through the courage of its people.
quote: Mero desh, timi feri uthnu chha.
fullPoem:
Sattako bhojma,
Bhokai baseko mero desh,
Aaja,
Sahidko laashmathi ubhiyeko loktantrama
Bacheko chha.

Sattako darbar,
Ra bahira ubhiyeko,
Bhokko bhirbich,
Khaithe mero desh.

Deshle bheka ghau pani,
Aba,
Muskurāuna sikun.

Ghamle khaithe paayeko,
Mero deshko mutu,
Aaja,
Andhyaro raat,
Ra nirashaka barshaharu.

Aashako chahile chirdai gayeko,
Pichhada bij,
Nilo aakash chhumne gantabyasamma pugos.

Mero desh,
Timi feri uthnu chha.

Andhyara raat,
Ra ragatle dhakeka,
Purana parkhaharu,
Aaja,
Timrai yuwaharuko aagole
Ujyalo maarg banaudai chhan.

Mero desh,
Timi feri uthnu chha.
meaning: This poem acknowledges the pain of inequality, sacrifice, and disappointment while refusing to surrender hope. It recognizes the lives that have been lost and the hardships many people continue to face, yet ultimately believes that a nation's future is shaped by the courage, compassion, and determination of its people. It is a poem of remembrance as much as it is a call to rebuild.
authorNotes: I wrote this because I wanted to express both grief and hope for my country. It is easy to focus only on what is broken, but I also wanted to believe that every generation has the ability to build something kinder than the one it inherited.
inspiration: The sacrifices made throughout Nepal's history, the resilience of ordinary people, and the hope that future generations will create a more just and compassionate nation.
seoTitle: Mero Desh, Timi Feri Uthnu Chha | A Nepali Spoken-Word Poem of Hope
seoDescription: A hopeful Nepali spoken-word poem reflecting on sacrifice, resilience, and the belief that Nepal can rise through the strength of its people.
altText: The Nepali flag rising against a sunrise over the Himalayas, symbolizing resilience, remembrance, and hope.

title: It Never Did
subtitle: The universe does not remember us forever, yet our moments still matter.
caption: A spoken-word meditation on mortality, memory, and the quiet realization that permanence was never what made life meaningful.
quote: Your favorite song will still survive, but not because of you. It never did.
fullPoem:
Have you ever wondered...
How many of your loved ones will come to your funeral?

Through your eyes,
I see.

The day you will die,
The universe won't even blink once.

Why would it?

The strangers will be laughing
On the same road
Your coffin just passed by.

Your name
Will be spoken less
In a year.

Existence
Will be reduced
From presence,
To photographs,
From photographs,
To rumor,
Then,
From rumors,
To nothing at all.

Within three generations,
You'll be biologically irrelevant.

Your great-grandchildren
Will not know your voice.

Within five generations,
Your existence
Will vanish from conversation.

Within ten,
Even your genetic influence
Will be diluted.

But,
There will still be
A few people,
Who will
Sadden briefly,
Till grief will grow tired of itself.

Your favorite song
Will still survive,
But not because of you.

It never did.

Through your eyes,
I see.
The smile
You bring to me.
meaning: This poem reflects on the impermanence of human life. It reminds us that names fade, memories disappear, and even grief eventually softens. Rather than presenting this as something hopeless, the poem quietly suggests that our value never depended on being remembered forever. Life gains meaning through the moments we share, the kindness we leave behind, and the people whose lives we touch while we are here.
authorNotes: I wrote this after thinking about how much energy we spend trying to leave a permanent mark on the world. The poem isn't about hopelessness; it's about accepting impermanence and realizing that perhaps we were never meant to last forever. We were only meant to live sincerely.
inspiration: Thoughts on mortality, the passage of time, the fading of memory across generations, and the quiet beauty of accepting that nothing lasts forever.
seoTitle: It Never Did | A Spoken-Word Poem on Mortality and Memory
seoDescription: A reflective spoken-word poem exploring death, memory, impermanence, and the quiet beauty of living despite being forgotten.
altText: A solitary figure beneath a vast night sky, symbolizing mortality, memory, and the passage of time.
